summary: Core loop = request deploy → lead approves/rejects → audit record written. MVP = f1 request, f2 approve/reject, f3 audit view. Notifications (f4) deferred to Next so the MVP fits the 1-3 month window. Top product risk: slow approvals push engineers to route around the tool (r1).

features.name: Request a deploy
features.user_story: As an engineer (u1), I request a deploy of a service to production so that it is queued for approval instead of shipping silently.

features.name: Approve or reject
features.user_story: As a team lead (u2), I approve or reject a pending request with a reason so that only sanctioned deploys proceed.

features.name: Audit trail view
features.user_story: As a team lead (u2), I browse the history of requests and decisions so that incident review can see who authorized a release.

features.name: Notifications
features.user_story: As a team lead (u2), I am notified of a pending request so that I decide quickly.

features.name: Per-service approver policies
features.user_story: As an admin, I configure which leads can approve which services.

mvp.cut_rationale: The core loop (request → decide → record) delivers g1 and g2 on its own and moves s1 to 100%. Notifications (f4) improve s2 but are not required to ship the loop, so they wait for Next (brief.timeline.t1). Per-service policies (f5) are configurability the MVP does not need (mvp-rules #4).

roadmap.outcome: every prod deploy now has an approval and an audit record
faster decisions — pushes median approval under 30 min (s2)

product_risks.risk: slow approvals cause engineers to bypass the tool, leaving audit gaps
product_risks.mitigation: keep the request flow to one screen; prioritize f4 notifications in Next

product_risks.risk: if a lead is unavailable, deploys stall
product_risks.mitigation: allow any lead of the service to approve (not a single named owner)
